text: Mira, that broke the spell. She’d made that same absurd comment to you, and I couldn’t stop laughing that time you quoted it to me. “Gran can’t have been serious,” I remember telling you. You’re right, Sis. It wasn’t funny. When Gran said the same to me, I felt like she was another person. Her face was perfectly blank. Her eyes seemed empty, her mouth tight like she would never smile again. That’s what you saw, too, I realize now. I should have supported you sooner when you talked about getting her help. Should have encouraged your plan to move Gran out of that apartment, at least. But I was in denial. Tell me: did those uninvited guests pack themselves into her suitcases and travel with her to your house? Did she see the Cabinet People again, once she’d moved to your place? Or did you notice if her eyes darted suspiciously to the cabinets in your remodeled kitchen? —the ones over the counter, or the larger ones beneath the sink where two of those people might fit, their limbs tangled around bends in the p-trap plumbing tubes. She never reached for a steak knife and held it tight to her chest with both frail hands, listening for a scratch behind wood, waiting for a door to spring open? No. Of course not. Gran slept better in those final months with you. We both have to believe that. I can’t say what got me on this train of thought. Mostly my weird experience last month when I walked into the wrong apartment, and the logical part of my mind seemed to switch off. Same with Gran, right? There’s no way those Cabinet People could have gotten into her apartment. Even if they did, they wouldn’t logically be able to fit with the other items in the full cupboards, not to mention the dividing shelves. And why wouldn’t they prefer more spacious living quarters? Seriously, how would they sleep comfortably? When they were awake, how would they amuse themselves? My reason for calling today might have something to do with a dream I had. Before bedtime last night, I’d been watching a magic show on television. The same program was playing in my dream. Yeah, all the money I spend on cable and streaming, and even my dreams have repeats. Eventually we got the ol’ saw-the-lady-in-half trick. Slightly more fun than a card trick, but pretty easy to figure out the secret. The assistant’s super thin, and super nimble, and the box is bigger than the audience realizes. The saw’s not too sharp or solid, either. In my dream, the assistant is a normal sized woman, and the box seems especially narrow. She climbs inside with the magician’s help, and it’s a tight fit. He closes the lid. Her head sticks out one end, long dark hair hanging down, nearly touching the stage. Bare feet stick out the other end, toes wriggling as if being tickled. They look real. The magician has pale skin and the kind of salt-and-pepper goatee favored by morticians in low-budget movies. He wears the typical magician’s tux with a cape flowing behind—hidden pockets everywhere, I’m sure, to conceal rabbits and doves and endless flowing scarves. The magician grabs at the air, and a handsaw appears. He bends the blade, making it sing. There’s a notch on the side of the box, indicating the midpoint where the saw should go. As he steps over to the boxed assistant, her head turns and she starts to struggle. “There’s someone in here with me.” The magician positions himself behind the box, then lowers the saw into the notch. “Don’t give away the secret.” He flicks the cape behind him with a flourish. Begins sawing. The woman starts screaming, her hair lashing the stage floor as she twists her neck. “Someone’s crawling over me,” she says. It occurs to me that the box is like a coffin. A coffin is a cabinet that fits into the ground. The hinged lid, once closed, should never be opened. If there’s another person in the magic box, the two of them must be twisted together like bread ties. Like braided bodies. I’m dreaming, but I can’t escape my lockdown paranoia. They are too close together, sweating their disease into each other pores. The saw moves back and forth. “Which one of us,” the assistant cries out. “Which one of us are you cutting?” That’s when I woke up. My bedroom was dark and stuffy. I couldn’t see or breathe or move—as if a lid pressed down on me, holding me in place. I heard the scratch of a saw’s teeth on wood. Fingernails digging into the grain. When did I get out of that box, Mira? It’s hard to say, since I might have been asleep for part of the time. Even after I was fully awake, my mind remained fuzzy. I turned on the light, walked from one room to the next, and couldn’t shake the feeling that something had changed. Like it wasn’t my apartment anymore. Or, at least, not only my apartment. I haven’t opened them, Mira. Not any of them. I’m afraid to. Tell me again, please. Gran stopped seeing the Cabinet People once she moved in with you. Didn’t she? In my experience, not many writers can successfully mix dashes of Lovecraft's pulpy cosmic horror with pulpy crime/noir sensibilities. The late great Tom Piccirilli comes to mind as one of the best at it. As you've already seen in this collection, Richard Thomas is more than capable of such a lethal stew, and so is Anthony J. Rapino, as he closes out the first installment of Liminal Spaces with his own spin on organized crime and watery abominations in “The Black Door.” THE BLACK DOOR by Anthony J. Rapino V anessa wanted to be convinced. I could tell by her eyes. “Will it hurt?” The corner of her mouth twitched. A smile’s teasing presence. “That’s the whole point.”
